title: Demand for bigger low-paid wage rise
full_text: Other Editions 01 May 2026 TheNation Demand for bigger low-paid wage rise The Australian EWIN HANNAN, WORKPLACE EDITOR 30 April 2026 647 words English AUSTLN 4 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. The ACTU has declared the spike in inflation strengthens the union movement’s decision to lift its wage claim for 3 million low-paid workers beyond 5 per cent at the forthcoming annual wage review. With headline inflation rising to 4.6 per cent as automotive fuel prices jumped 32.8 per cent from February to March, ACTU president Michele O’Neil said soaring petrol costs were on the brink of wiping out the entire wage claim. “Workers are getting squeezed at the bowser, at the checkout and when they pay their bills,” Ms O’Neil said. “Today’s inflation figures strengthen the union movement’s case for stronger minimum-wage increases and vehicle allowances that keep up with the cost of living. “Working people should not go backwards because of a conflict overseas. They were not responsible for petrol prices surging more than 30 per cent in a month, and they should not be punished for that with a real wage cut.” The Australian first reported two weeks ago that unions had decided to increase their $47.40-a-week claim before the Fair Work Commission, calling on the Albanese government to back the bid. The upgraded ACTU claim will be opposed by business groups that want a 3.5 per cent rise paid to 2.6 million award-reliant workers as well as 500,000 employees covered by enterprise agreements that tie wage rises to the annual wage decision, including 200,000 workers at supermarket giants Coles and Woolworths. The government announced in March it would back an above-inflation pay rise for award-reliant workers, prompting employer claims it was undermining the fight against rising inflation. Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry chief executive Andrew McKellar said on Wednesday that the 4.6 per cent inflation figure showed the pain businesses were experiencing from rising costs. “The pressures on businesses from rising inflation makes it all that more important the Fair Work Commissions takes a measured approach in deciding any increase in the minimum wage when it hands down its decision next month,” Mr McKellar said. Australian Industry Group chief executive Innes Willox said fuel prices had pushed inflation to “intolerable levels” and would worsen as the impact on plastics, fertilisers, construction materials and transport appeared in April and May “In the months ahead, household incomes will sink and business costs will dramatically rise, just as they did in 2022,” Mr Willox said. “But unlike 2022, the economy is not on the upswing. The energy crisis will have major impacts across industry that clearly dampen economic activity in the time ahead. Stagflation is no longer a risk to be avoided, but a reality to be managed. “The RBA now faces a difficult choice when it meets next Tuesday. It is really Hobson’s choice, where there is only one alternative. With inflation well outside the target band and worse to come, it has little choice but to raise rates. But with the economy sure to weaken, this risks pushing us closer to recession.” Ms O’Neil said unions would be watching closely for any companies using rising costs as cover to price gouge. “Workers do not want another situation where big companies profit during a war while everyone else is told to tighten their belts,” the ACTU president said. “Australians remember supermarkets using the cover of inflation during the pandemic to raise their prices and fatten their profits. We do not want a repeat of that sort of greedy profiteering. “Without a fair increase to vehicle allowances, fast food delivery drivers, aged care workers and disability support workers will continue paying out of pocket for using their own vehicle just to do their job. We need to see the fuel allowance for workers go up urgently.” The Australian-20260430-Australian-4-000500533949 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The ACTU is pushing for a wage increase for 3 million low-paid workers, raising their claim to over 5% due to a spike in inflation driven by rising fuel prices. This move is opposed by business groups advocating for a smaller 3.5% increase. The situation highlights the ongoing tension between wage growth and inflationary pressures in the Australian economy.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: ACTU demands over 5% wage rise for low-paid workers due to inflation
Business groups advocate for a 3.5% increase instead
Rising fuel prices are significantly impacting cost of living and wage negotiations
relevance: Understanding wage dynamics and inflation is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts economic forecasts and financial planning.
